ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for the complete Bun Dau Mam Tom dish. FOODON_03315671 (fried tofu food product) is used as the closest parent anchor for the tofu component. The fermented shrimp paste condiment and rice vermicelli components are not individually coded here.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (Soyabean curd / tofu) is used as the closest parent. No composite FoodEx2 code exists for the full Bun Dau Mam Tom dish including fermented shrimp paste and rice vermicelli.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Bun Dau Mam Tom or fried tofu with fermented shrimp paste as a composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Bun Dau Mam Tom is a shared platter of approximately 450 g, comprising blocks of deep-fried tofu, a portion of rice vermicelli, fresh herbs, and a small bowl of fermented shrimp paste dipping sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Tofu, fried, and rice vermicelli composite estimate based on USDA FoodData Central entries: Tofu, fried (FDC ID 172443) and Rice noodles, cooked (FDC ID 169730), with fermented shrimp paste component referenced from FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for the complete Bun Dau Mam Tom dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted composite: approximately 40% deep-fried tofu (USDA FDC 172443), 35% cooked rice vermicelli (USDA FDC 169730), 10% fermented shrimp paste (FAO/INFOODS SEA), and 15% fresh herbs and condiments (negligible macro contribution). Per-100g values reflect the blended edible portion of the platter.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — rice noodles and tofu composite analogous estimate; rice noodles GI ~53 (Atkinson et al., 2021, Am J Clin Nutr); tofu GI ~15 (University of Sydney GI Database)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Bun Dau Mam Tom. GI estimated as weighted composite: rice vermicelli component GI ~53 (Atkinson et al. 2021, Am J Clin Nutr 114:1625-1632; University of Sydney GI Database), fried tofu component GI ~15 (University of Sydney GI Database). Weighted blend at approximately 35% rice vermicelli and 40% tofu by mass yields an estimated composite GI of ~52, classified as low. Glycemic load per 450 g serving calculated as (52 × 65.25 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = ~34.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium from fermented shrimp paste (mam tom)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid Bun Dau Mam Tom due to the high sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g, primarily from fermented shrimp paste), which can significantly contribute to daily sodium intake and elevate blood pressure; requesting reduced dipping sauce or substituting with a lower-sodium condiment is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index from rice vermicelli component
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should be mindful of the rice vermicelli portion of Bun Dau Mam Tom, as it contributes a moderate glycemic load (~34 per full serving); pairing with the high-protein fried tofu and fresh herbs can help blunt the postprandial glucose response, but portion control of the vermicelli is recommended.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and deep-frying oil content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, the high sodium content from fermented shrimp paste and the fat from deep-frying raise concern; the soy-based tofu provides beneficial plant protein and isoflavones, but the dish should be consumed in moderation with attention to the dipping sauce quantity and overall saturated fat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bun Dau Mam Tom (Bún Đậu Mắm Tôm) is a classic Northern Vietnamese street food dish consisting of deep-fried firm tofu, cold rice vermicelli noodles (bun), and an assortment of fresh herbs such as perilla (tia to), Vietnamese mint (rau hung), and sliced cucumber, all served alongside a pungent fermented shrimp paste dipping sauce (mam tom) that is typically seasoned with lime juice, sugar, and chilli. The tofu is fried until golden and crispy on the outside while remaining soft inside, and the dish is assembled as a communal platter meant for sharing. The defining flavour element is the mam tom — a deeply savoury, intensely umami, and pungent purple-grey paste made from fermented shrimp, which is an acquired taste central to the dish's identity. Nutritionally, the dish provides moderate energy (~148 kcal per 100 g), meaningful plant protein from soy tofu (~7.2 g per 100 g), and calcium (~120 mg per 100 g). The primary clinical concern is the high sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g) driven by the fermented shrimp paste, making it a high-sodium dish relevant to hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 52 (low-to-moderate), with the rice vermicelli contributing the majority of glycemic load. The dish is not halal due to the fermented shrimp paste and is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans. In Singapore, it is served at Vietnamese restaurants such as Thien Long Vietnam Restaurant and represents an authentic Northern Vietnamese culinary tradition increasingly available in the Southeast Asian diaspora food scene.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for the complete Bun Dau Mam Tom dish. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central entries for fried tofu and cooked rice vermicelli, supplemented by FAO/INFOODS SEA data for fermented shrimp paste. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database values for the component foods. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level sources are high-quality approved databases and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central entries for fried tofu or rice vermicelli, and no reliable value is available from FAO/INFOODS SEA for this composite. 2. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for the tofu and rice vermicelli components; no hydrogenated oils are used in traditional preparation). 3. The food_group in the input JSON was listed as 'Fish and seafood', which is inaccurate for this dish; it has been corrected to 'Mixed' as the dish is a composite of tofu (soy), rice vermicelli, and fermented shrimp paste. 4. The cuisine in the input JSON was listed as 'Singapore'; this has been corrected to 'Vietnamese' as Bun Dau Mam Tom is a Vietnamese dish served in Singapore. Multilingual naming follows the Vietnamese cuisine rule (Vietnamese native-script name mandatory) plus Singaporean context names (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) given the Singapore serving context. 5. The restaurant price of SGD 32.00 for a single restaurant (Thien Long Vietnam Restaurant, Circular Road) is noted from the source record but is not used as a nutritional data point. 6. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI 52 × available carbohydrate 65.25 g per 450 g serving) / 100 ≈ 34. 7. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data and should be treated as approximate. 8. The dish is not halal as mam tom (fermented shrimp paste) may contain non-halal processing agents and the restaurant is not certified halal.
